Kingdom.name: {=PjO7oY16}Vale of Arryn
Kingdom.title: {=iYCR3xuQ}Vale of Arryn
Kingdom.text: The Vale of Arryn is a mountainous region of Westeros bordered by the Mountains of the Moon and the Narrow Sea. It is ruled by House Arryn from the Eyrie, a fortress situated high in the mountains. Access into the Vale from the west is controlled by the Bloody Gate. The Vale contains fertile lowlands and several notable houses sworn to House Arryn, including Houses Royce, Corbray, Waynwood, and Redfort. Its only major ports are Gulltown, located on the Narrow Sea and Sisterton located in the Sister Isles.

Kingdom.name: {=PjO7oY16}Crownlands
Kingdom.title: {=iYCR3xuQ}Iron Throne
Kingdom.text: The Crownlands are the lands directly ruled by the Iron Throne, stretching along the eastern coast of Westeros around Blackwater Bay. At their heart lies King’s Landing, the capital of the Seven Kingdoms, dominated by the Red Keep atop Aegon’s High Hill. The region also includes the ancient island fortress of Dragonstone, ancestral seat of House Targaryen, along with notable castles such as Rosby, Stokeworth, Duskendale, Rook’s Rest, and Crackclaw Point. Unlike the older kingdoms, the Crownlands were never a unified realm before Aegon’s Conquest, instead consisting of riverlords, stormlords, and petty kings whose territories were claimed and reorganized under Targaryen rule. Culturally, the Crownlands are diverse, reflecting centuries of migration, royal influence, and proximity to the capital. The population ranges from wealthy courtiers and merchants in King’s Landing to hardy fisherfolk along the coasts and the fiercely independent people of Crackclaw Point, who retain ancient First Men traditions. Politically, the region’s importance far outweighs its size, as control of the Crownlands means control of the royal court, the ports of Blackwater Bay, and the seat of power itself. Armies marching on the Iron Throne must pass through these lands, making the Crownlands both the most contested and the most strategically vital territory in Westeros.

Kingdom.name: {=PjO7oY16}Stormlands
Kingdom.title: {=iYCR3xuQ}Stormlands
Kingdom.text: The Crownlands are the lands directly ruled by the Iron Throne, stretching along the eastern coast of Westeros around Blackwater Bay. At their heart lies King’s Landing, the capital of the Seven Kingdoms, dominated by the Red Keep atop Aegon’s High Hill. The region also includes the ancient island fortress of Dragonstone, ancestral seat of House Targaryen, along with notable castles such as Rosby, Stokeworth, Duskendale, Rook’s Rest, and Crackclaw Point. Unlike the older kingdoms, the Crownlands were never a unified realm before Aegon’s Conquest, instead consisting of riverlords, stormlords, and petty kings whose territories were claimed and reorganized under Targaryen rule. Culturally, the Crownlands are diverse, reflecting centuries of migration, royal influence, and proximity to the capital. The population ranges from wealthy courtiers and merchants in King’s Landing to hardy fisherfolk along the coasts and the fiercely independent people of Crackclaw Point, who retain ancient First Men traditions. Politically, the region’s importance far outweighs its size, as control of the Crownlands means control of the royal court, the ports of Blackwater Bay, and the seat of power itself. Armies marching on the Iron Throne must pass through these lands, making the Crownlands both the most contested and the most strategically vital territory in Westeros.

Kingdom.name: {=PjO7oY16}Riverlands
Kingdom.title: {=iYCR3xuQ}Riverlands
Kingdom.text: The Riverlands sit at the heart of Westeros, a fertile region defined by the great waterways that give it its name. The land is shaped by the Trident, a mighty river system formed by the Red Fork, Green Fork, and Blue Fork, whose tributaries water broad fields, orchards, and pastures. Because of its central location and open terrain, the Riverlands have long been vulnerable to invasion. Armies marching between the north, west, crownlands, and stormlands often pass through its lands, and its castles and towns frequently become battlegrounds during times of war. For centuries the Riverlands were ruled by many different kings before falling under the control of the ironborn. After Aegon I Targaryen’s Conquest, the region was granted to House Tully of Riverrun, who were raised as Lords Paramount of the Trident. From Riverrun, House Tully commands a powerful network of bannermen including Houses Blackwood, Bracken, Frey, Mallister, and others. By the reign of Daeron II Targaryen, the Riverlands remain a wealthy but often contested realm, teeming full of working class guilds, defined by its fertile lands, strong river castles, and the rivalries that have shaped its history.

Kingdom.name: {=PjO7oY16}Westerlands
Kingdom.title: {=iYCR3xuQ}Westerlands
Kingdom.text: The Westerlands lie along the western coast of the continent, a rugged region of steep hills, rocky shores, and rich mineral wealth. Beneath its mountains and ridges are some of the richest gold and silver mines in the known world, and this wealth has long made the Westerlands one of the most powerful realms in the Seven Kingdoms. The region is ruled from Casterly Rock, the ancient seat of House Lannister, whose authority extends over a network of fortified castles, prosperous market towns, and productive mines. The great port city of Lannisport, lying beneath the Rock, serves as the region’s commercial heart and one of the largest cities in the realm. For thousands of years the Westerlands were ruled by the Kings of the Rock, first the legendary House Casterly and later House Lannister, who took the Rock through the cunning of Lann the Clever. Like the other kingdoms, the Westerlands were brought into the realm during Aegon’s Conquest, when King Loren Lannister bent the knee after the Field of Fire. By the reign of King Daeron II Targaryen, the Westerlands remain wealthy and influential, their lords commanding vast resources drawn from their mines and trade. The banners of houses such as Marbrand, Lefford, Crakehall, and Reyne stand beneath the golden lion, forming a powerful western bloc within the Seven Kingdoms as tensions quietly grow across the realm.

Kingdom.name: {=PjO7oY16}Reach
Kingdom.title: {=iYCR3xuQ}Reach
Kingdom.text: The Reach is the most fertile and populous of the Seven Kingdoms, a land of broad rivers, rolling fields, and prosperous market towns stretching across the southern heart of the realm. Vast harvests of grain, fruit, and wine flow from its fertile soil, feeding much of the continent and supporting a dense network of castles and thriving settlements. At its center stands Highgarden, the seat of House Tyrell, whose rule extends over some of the richest lands in Westeros. The Reach is also home to the great city of Oldtown, one of the oldest and largest cities in the realm, where the Citadel trains the maesters and the Starry Sept long served as the center of the Faith. Before the coming of the dragons, the Reach was ruled for thousands of years by the Gardeners, the legendary Kings of the Reach, until their line ended at the Field of Fire during Aegon's Conquest. In the aftermath, Aegon I Targaryen raised House Tyrell, former stewards of Highgarden, to rule the region as Lords Paramount of the Reach. By the reign of King Daeron II Targaryen, the Reach remains one of the most powerful regions of the Seven Kingdoms, able to field vast armies drawn from the banners of houses such as Hightower, Redwyne, Tarly, Rowan, and Florent, whose wealth and strength make the Reach a cornerstone of the realm's power.

Kingdom.name: {=PjO7oY16}Dorne
Kingdom.title: {=iYCR3xuQ}Principality of Dorne
Kingdom.text: Dorne is the southernmost of the Seven Kingdoms, a harsh and diverse land of deserts, mountains, and fertile river valleys shaped by heat and isolation. Unlike the other realms, Dorne was not conquered during Aegon’s Conquest, instead joining the realm peacefully through marriage during the reign of King Daeron II Targaryen, securing its status as a principality rather than a kingdom. It is ruled from Sunspear by House Martell, whose authority extends across the deserts, the Red Mountains, and the rich lands along the Greenblood, where Rhoynar traditions remain strong. Dornish culture differs markedly from the rest of the realm, with equal inheritance laws and deep-rooted customs brought by the Rhoynar. By the time of Daeron II, Dorne stands as a distinct and influential part of the realm, its princes maintaining their ancient titles and autonomy while bound to the Iron Throne.

Kingdom.name: {=PjO7oY16}North
Kingdom.title: {=iYCR3xuQ}North
Kingdom.text: The North is the largest of the Seven Kingdoms, a vast and rugged land of dense forests, sweeping plains, and frozen tundra stretching from the Neck to the Wall. Its harsh climate and long winters shape both the land and its people, who are known for their resilience and adherence to ancient traditions. The region is ruled from Winterfell by House Stark, whose authority extends across great distances through a network of ancient bannermen such as House Umber, House Karstark, House Manderly, and House Bolton. Before the coming of the dragons, the North was ruled for thousands of years by the Kings of Winter of House Stark, until King Torrhen Stark bent the knee during Aegon’s Conquest, bringing the region into the realm without war. By the reign of King Daeron II Targaryen, the North remains a distinct and largely self-contained land, where the worship of the Old Gods endures and the ancient ways still define its people.

Kingdom.name: {=PjO7oY16}Skagos
Kingdom.title: {=iYCR3xuQ}Skagos
Kingdom.text: Skagos stands in open rebellion beneath the rule of House Magnar, its lord casting aside fealty to Winterfell and proclaiming himself against the dominion of House Stark. The island, harsh and mountainous, is long known for its fierce and insular peoples, where the old ways hold strong and the authority of the mainland has ever been tenuous. From the stone fastnesses and cold shores of Skagos, the Magnar calls his banners, gathering clans and captains beneath him, emboldened by distance and defiance. Trade and tribute are cast off, and the narrow seas about the island grow perilous, as ships loyal to the rebel lord harry northern waters. Though the Starks have long claimed lordship over Skagos, its submission has never been absolute, and now, in this rising, the island once more asserts its will, threatening to draw the North into conflict against one of its most unruly and ancient domains.

Kingdom.name: {=PjO7oY16}Iron Islands
Kingdom.title: {=iYCR3xuQ}Iron Islands
Kingdom.text: The Iron Islands lie upon the Sunset Sea, a harsh and wind-lashed chain of rocky isles where the rule of House Greyjoy of Pyke is upheld through the ancient customs of the ironborn. Sparse in fertile land yet rich in seafaring strength, the isles have long bred a people hardened by storm and salt, whose reavers once ranged far across the coasts of Westeros and beyond. Though brought to heel after the fall of the last kings of the isles, the ironborn still hold to their old traditions, valuing strength, conquest, and the taking of wealth from distant shores. From Pyke and the lesser isles of Harlaw, Great Wyk, and Orkmont, their captains and lords command longships that remain ever ready for war, and though they bend the knee in name, the spirit of the old way endures, shaping a realm ever restless beneath the rule of the Iron Throne.

Kingdom.name: {=PjO7oY16}Night's Watch
Kingdom.title: {=iYCR3xuQ}Night's Watch
Kingdom.text: The Night's Watch stands as the northernmost order of the realm, sworn to guard the Wall and defend the Seven Kingdoms against raiders, wildlings, and darker things said to dwell beyond the Wall. Though diminished from the strength it possessed in ages past, the Watch still holds Castle Black, the Shadow Tower, Westwatch-by-the-Bridge and Eastwatch-by-the-Sea, while the ruined castles between them stand silent along the Wall's great length with only Oakenshield, Deep Lake and Stonedoor remaining garrisoned. Its brothers come from every corner of the realm, whether younger sons, criminals, or men seeking honor, all surrendering lands, titles, and inheritance upon taking the black. In the year 196 AC the order is led by Jack Musgood, known to his brothers as Jolly Jack, a commander known for good humor and easy fellowship. Though the Night’s Watch takes no part in the quarrels of the realm, its strength remains a matter of great importance to the North, for the Wall endures as the last defense of the kingdoms of men.
